title: Catamaran or Monohull: How to Pick the Right Boat for Your Trip
topic: Catamaran vs monohull: choosing the right boat to rent
html:
One of the first questions every traveller faces when planning a day on the water is whether to rent a catamaran or a monohull. Both will carry you to a beautiful anchorage, but they behave very differently, and the right choice depends on who is coming along, what you want to do, and how the conditions are likely to look. This guide breaks down the practical differences so you can rent a boat that genuinely suits your trip rather than one that simply looks good in the photos.

The basic difference between the two hulls

A monohull is the classic boat shape: a single hull that sits deep in the water and leans, or heels, as it moves under sail or turns at speed. A catamaran has two parallel hulls joined by a wide deck, so it stays much flatter and floats higher. That single structural difference shapes nearly everything else, from how stable the boat feels to how much living space you get and where you can drop anchor.

Stability and comfort on board

For most casual renters, stability is the headline issue. A catamaran stays remarkably level, which makes moving around easy and tends to reduce seasickness. That makes it a popular choice for families with young children, mixed groups, and anyone nervous about being on the water. Meals on deck stay put, drinks do not slide off the table, and guests who have never sailed before usually feel at ease within minutes.

A monohull, by contrast, leans into the wind and rolls more with the waves. Many sailors love exactly that sensation because it feels alive and connected to the sea. If part of your group wants a genuine sailing experience rather than a floating terrace, a monohull delivers it. Picture how your particular guests will feel after a few hours aboard, not just during the first calm half hour.

Space, layout and privacy

Catamarans win on sheer volume. The wide deck creates a large saloon, a generous cockpit, and cabins spread across two hulls, which gives couples and families more privacy. There is usually plenty of room to lounge, sunbathe, and gather for a meal without anyone feeling crowded. For a celebration, a charter with friends, or a multi-day trip, that extra space is hard to beat.

Monohulls offer a cosier, more traditional layout. Cabins are closer together and the interior feels snug rather than sprawling, which suits smaller groups and couples who prefer intimacy to acreage. A monohull of a given length will almost always cost less to hire than a catamaran of the same length, so if budget matters and your group is small, it can be the smarter pick when you hire a boat for the day or the week.

Performance, anchoring and where you can go

Performance depends on what you value. Catamarans are fast on a straight, downwind run and they draw very little water, so they can tuck into shallow bays and creep close to a beach where deeper boats cannot follow. They are harder to manoeuvre in a tight marina, however, and they need a wider berth. Monohulls point higher into the wind, handle rough seas with a predictable motion, and slot neatly into standard berths, though their deeper keel keeps them out of the shallowest spots.

A simple rule of thumb: choose a catamaran for space, calm motion, and shallow anchorages, and a monohull for traditional sailing feel, tighter budgets, and confident upwind sailing.

Matching the boat to your trip

Think honestly about the day you actually want. A relaxed swim-and-lunch outing with non-sailors leans towards a catamaran. A spirited afternoon learning to trim sails leans towards a monohull. Consider these quick prompts before you book a yacht charter:

  • Group size: larger groups usually appreciate a catamaran's deck space.
  • Seasickness: nervous stomachs are happier on a level multihull.
  • Budget: for the same length, a monohull is generally cheaper.
  • Conditions: open, choppy water can favour a monohull's sea motion.
  • Anchorages: shallow, sandy bays suit a shallow-draught catamaran.

The bottom line

There is no universally better boat, only the better boat for your particular day. Decide on the experience first, then let the hull follow from that. Whichever way you lean, comparing a wide range of options side by side is the fastest route to a confident decision, and a marketplace like boathire24.com lets you weigh catamarans and monohulls in the same place. Owners on the supply side can do the reverse and reach the right travellers, too, since it is straightforward to list your boat and put an idle hull to work.
